text: So this would seem a rather straightforward laparoscopic high anterior resection for a locally advanced distal sigmoid proximal rectal cancer. But what made this case a bit more challenging are the adhesions from his previous laparotomy. So At the start of the operation we were standing on the left side of the patient taking down all these adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall to facilitate safe entry of the rest of the ports. Another challenging aspect about this case really is that this guy has a long and loopy sigmoid colon as you can see in the CAT scan. that's fairly stuck to the right side. This has to be straightened up before you can actually begin with the standard steps of the operation. So in our hands, the monopolar coterie seems to work best in lies in this adhesions

The surgeon moves to the right side of the patient to begin the neutral to lateral resection, opening the peritoneum widely. The dissection targets the inferior mesenteric pedicle, revealing its smooth and shiny surface. A sizable lymph node is visible in the pyramus enteric pedicle, which needs to be removed en bloc. The dissection proceeds behind the pedicle towards the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, preparing it for skeletonization and application of hemo locks.

The surgeon identifies a sizable lymph node in the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) chain and removes it with appropriate margins. After skeletonizing the artery down to the adventitia level, hemolux clips are applied to secure the vessel, which is then transected high. The cut end of the mesenteric pedicle is grasped to facilitate further medial to lateral dissection.

The surgeon begins pelvic dissection, identifying the inferior hypogastric nerve and aiming to preserve it while following the areolar tissues into the deep pelvis. The visceretum is carefully pushed upward to expose the dissection plane, using a combination of sharp and blunt techniques to maintain anatomical structures.

The surgeon continues the dissection on the lateral side, removing adhesions to expose the left gutter up to the urinary bladder. The peritoneum is being unzipped, with the left ureter visible and safely away from the surgical field. Dissection proceeds close to the mesorectum to avoid lateral sidewall nerves. The area near the tumor is being dissected, with attention to a potential defect near the urinary bladder. The surgeon follows areolar tissue planes to guide the dissection path.

The surgeon uses a colonoscope to accurately localize the tumor within the colon. The dissection proceeds distally to achieve a distal transection margin, measuring five centimeters, with a perpendicular cut across the identified point.

The surgeon focuses on the pyramus enteric vein, located just below the border of the pancreas, and proceeds to skeletonize it, ensuring no other vessels are present. The mesentery is trimmed to facilitate extraction through the fan solid sessions.

The surgeon uses an open stapler to transect the distal margin in one motion, preparing for the colorectal anastomosis. The anastomosis is performed in the standard manner, followed by a jacuzzi test using a colonoscope to check the integrity of the anastomosis.
